_note: Hand-curated WHITELIST for the Expressions layer (pedagogy plan P3). Keyed by the exact `he` string as authored in the source lessons; gen-expressions.mjs joins on it and FAILS on any key that no longer matches a source (so a lesson edit can never silently drop an expression). Only entries listed here ship: the source pool also holds textbook-invented or mistyped 'idioms', and an expression an Israeli never says is worse than no expression. `usage` is the point of this whole layer — WHEN / WITH WHOM / register — and is never auto-generated. `literal` is filled only when the literal reading differs from the meaning (that gap IS the lesson). register: street | casual | neutral | formal | rude | dated.

categories.label: Street Hebrew
categories.blurb: Arabic loanwords. This is what you actually hear on the street, in the shuk, in a cab. Not one of these is in a textbook, and you cannot follow a normal conversation without them.

categories.label: The Yiddish Layer
categories.blurb: Ashkenazi Yiddish that survived into Israeli Hebrew. Carries affection, sarcasm and complaint — registers Hebrew itself is oddly bad at.

categories.label: Reactions
categories.blurb: What you say back. Fluency is mostly reacting at the right speed, not producing long sentences.

categories.label: Blessings & Wishes
categories.blurb: The ritual layer. Israelis say these constantly, secular ones included — they are social reflexes, not religious statements.

categories.label: Pushback
categories.blurb: Disagreeing without it being a fight. Israeli conversation is more direct than French or English; flat contradiction is normal and not rude.

categories.label: Small Talk & The Interrogation
categories.blurb: The opener, then the famously personal questions. An Israeli will ask your salary and your marital status within ten minutes. It reads as interest, not intrusion.

categories.label: Fillers & Hedges
categories.blurb: The words that buy you thinking time and make you sound like a speaker instead of a translator.

categories.label: Closing a Conversation
categories.blurb: Leaving well. Israelis close warm and vague — the exact goodbye matters less than the warmth.

categories.label: Body Idioms
categories.blurb: Heart, head, hand, mouth. The oldest idiom layer, shared with the Tanakh, still used daily.

categories.label: Animal Idioms
categories.blurb: Mostly transparent, and mostly identical to the English or French ones — free vocabulary.

categories.label: Food Idioms
categories.blurb: Bread, salt, milk and honey. Half biblical, half kitchen.

סַבַּבָּה.usage: The default yes/fine/cool. Answers 'how are you', accepts a plan, closes a deal. Safe with anyone under ~60; do not use with a clerk at Misrad Hapnim.

יַלָּה.usage: Let's go / hurry / enough. The single most used word in Israel. Also ends a conversation: 'yalla, bye'. Everyone, everywhere, all day.

וָואלָּה.usage: Genuine surprise, or 'huh, really'. Tone carries all of it — flat walla is polite acknowledgment, rising walla is real astonishment.

אַחְלָה.usage: Great / excellent, about a thing: achla place, achla guy. Interchangeable with sababa but rates quality rather than agreement.

מַבְּסוּט.usage: Content, pleased — usually said about someone else, often with a nod: 'he's mabsut'. Slightly older-generation.

חַ'בִּיבִּי.usage: Mate / bro, between men. Warm, or heavily sarcastic in an argument ('listen, habibi…') — same word, opposite temperature.

דּוּגְרִי.usage: Straight, no diplomacy. 'Dugri?' invites the blunt answer. A very Israeli value: you are asking to skip the padding.

יָאנִי.usage: I mean / sort of. Hedges a claim or fills a pause. Overusing it is a tell that you learned Hebrew on the street, which is a good problem.

חְ'לָאס.usage: Enough, done, drop it. Sharper than 'day'. Ends an argument, or a kid's tantrum.

מַ'נְיָאק.usage: A real insult, not banter — Arabic origin, vulgar at the root. Recognize it, do not deploy it.

נוּ.usage: The all-purpose prod: so? / well? / get on with it. Impatience, encouragement, or an eye-roll depending entirely on the vowel length.

חוּצְפָּה.usage: Nerve, gall. Half-condemnation half-admiration — Israelis are quietly proud of chutzpa as a national trait.

נֶבֶּעךְ.usage: Poor thing. Pity with a faint sigh, often at someone's misfortune you are not going to fix. Older speakers, or ironic among young ones.

נוּדְנִיק.usage: A pest — someone who will not stop asking. Affectionate about a child, less so about a colleague.

שְׁמֶנְדְּרִיק.usage: A hapless fool. Mild and almost fond; you would say it about a nephew, not a boss.

שׁלִימָאזֶל.usage: The chronically unlucky one. The classic pairing: the shlemiel spills the soup, the shlimazel is who it lands on.

מַה נִּשְׁמָע?.usage: The standard 'how are you'. Expects 'beseder' or 'hakol beseder', not an actual report on your life.

מַה הַמַּצָּב?.usage: Slightly more casual than ma nishma. Between friends, or a shopkeeper who knows you.

מָה הָעִנְיָנִים?.usage: What's going on. Friendly, a bit breezy — a peer, not a stranger.

מָה חָדָשׁ?.usage: What's new — for someone you have not seen in a while. Genuinely invites news, unlike ma nishma.

אֵיךְ הַחַיִּים?.usage: How's life. Warmer and broader; implies you have time to actually hear the answer.

אַתָּה חָדָשׁ פֹּה?.usage: Are you new here (m.). Prompted by your accent. Not a challenge — the follow-up is usually help.

לָמָּה עָלִיתָ אַרְצָה?.usage: Why did you make aliyah (m.). Asked sincerely and often, sometimes within minutes. A short honest answer lands better than a speech.

כַּמָּה זְמַן אַתָּה כָּאן?.usage: How long have you been here (m.). Follows 'where are you from' almost automatically.

אַתָּה נָשׂוּי?.usage: Are you married (m.). Asked by near-strangers without a hint of awkwardness. Frequently precedes an offer to introduce you to someone.

יֵשׁ לְךָ יְלָדִים?.usage: Do you have kids (m.). Same register as asking the time. Deflecting is what would seem strange.

בֶּן כַּמָּה אַתָּה?.usage: How old are you (m.). Normal here. 'Bat kama at?' to a woman — and no, it is not rude.

כַּמָּה אַתָּה מַרְוִיחַ?.usage: How much do you earn (m.). Genuinely asked, genuinely answered. If you would rather not: 'ze ishi miday'.

כַּמָּה שִׁלַּמְתָּ עַל הַדִּירָה?.usage: What did you pay for the apartment (m.). Real-estate prices are a national sport; expect the question and an opinion on your answer.

מָה אַתָּה חוֹשֵׁב עַל הַמֶּמְשָׁלָה?.usage: What do you think of the government (m.). Everyone has a view and will share it. Safe to have none out loud while you are new.

זֶה אִישִׁי מִדַּי.usage: That's too personal. The polite exit from the interrogation. Says it without offending — Israelis take it as a fair move.

אַתָּה שׁוֹאֵל הַרְבֵּה שְׁאֵלוֹת.usage: You ask a lot of questions (m.). Said with a smile it is banter, and usually earns respect rather than an apology.

הָעִבְרִית שֶׁלִּי עוֹד לֹא מֻשְׁלֶמֶת.usage: My Hebrew isn't perfect yet. Buys patience instantly. Better than apologizing — it states a fact and keeps the conversation in Hebrew.

דַּבֵּר לְאַט יוֹתֵר בְּבַקָּשָׁה.usage: Speak slower please (m.). The most useful sentence of your first year. Note the register: a bare imperative is blunt — softening it to "efshar ledaber le'at yoter?" (can you speak slower?) is what most Israelis would actually say to a stranger.

אֵין בְּעָיָה.usage: No problem. Accepts a request or waves off a mistake. The reflex answer to 'slicha'.

הַכֹּל בְּסֵדֶר.usage: All good. The default answer to ma nishma, and the default reassurance that nothing is wrong.

בְּסֵדֶר גָּמוּר.usage: Totally fine — an emphatic yes to a proposal. Warmer than plain 'beseder'.

יוֹפִי.usage: Great / nice one. Genuine to a child or a friend; from a bureaucrat with a flat tone it is pure sarcasm.

מְצוּיָּן.usage: Excellent. Neutral enough for work and a teacher, unlike sababa.

כָּל הַכָּבוֹד.usage: Well done — real respect for an achievement. Said to soldiers, to kids, to anyone who just did something hard.

עָבוֹדָה יָפָה.usage: Nice work. Workplace register, on a finished job.

חָבָל עַל הַזְּמָן.usage: Amazing — the literal reading is the exact opposite of the meaning. Tone decides: enthusiastic = incredible, flat and weary = genuinely a waste of time. The classic oleh trap.

אֵין דְּבָרִים כָּאֵלֶּה.usage: There's nothing like it — top-tier praise for food, a place, a performance.

אֵיזֶה כֵּיף!.usage: How fun! Unguarded delight — at plans, at good news. 'Keyf' has no clean English equivalent.

מַמָּשׁ!.usage: Totally / really! Agreement with force. Also 'mamash lo' = absolutely not.

בֶּאֱמֶת?.usage: Really? Plain curiosity. Sharpen the tone and it becomes doubt.

בְּדִיּוּק.usage: Exactly. Endorses what was just said. Also literal: 'be-diyuk be-shalosh' = at three sharp.

לֹא נוֹרָא.usage: It's fine, don't worry about it. The standard absolution after someone apologizes or breaks something small.

מַעַנְיֵן.usage: Interesting. Real interest, or the polite parking of something you disagree with — same as in English.

וָוּאוּ.usage: Wow. Borrowed straight from English and used exactly the same way.

מָה אַתָּה אוֹמֵר!.usage: You don't say! (m.) — surprise at news. Can be dry sarcasm if you already knew.

אֲנִי מֵבִין אוֹתְךָ.usage: I get you (m.). Signals comprehension and sympathy at once. 'Mevina' if you are a woman.

תַּכְלֶס.usage: Bottom line / honestly. Cuts to the point, or concedes one: 'tachles, you're right'. Yiddish by way of Hebrew tachlit.

דַּוְקָא.usage: Actually / on the contrary / deliberately. Notoriously hard for olim: it flags that reality contradicts the expectation. 'Davka tov' = actually good, despite what you assumed.

מַזָּל טוֹב.usage: Congratulations — for what already happened (birth, wedding, new job). Never a wish for the future, unlike the English 'good luck'.

בְּהַצְלָחָה.usage: Good luck — before the exam, the interview, the drive. The forward-looking counterpart to mazal tov.

בָּרוּךְ הַשֵּׁם.usage: Thank God. Constant, secular speakers included — it is closer to punctuation than to theology. A normal answer to ma nishma.

בְּעֶזְרַת הַשֵּׁם.literal: with the help of the Name
בְּעֶזְרַת הַשֵּׁם.usage: God willing — about the future. Religious speakers mean it; secular ones use it as a hedge, like inshallah.

בִּשְׁעָה טוֹבָה.usage: In due time — said to a pregnant woman, or about anything awaited. Superstition-adjacent: you avoid naming the date directly.

מָה פִּתְאוֹם?.usage: No way / since when? Rejects a premise outright. Also the warm refusal of an offer to pay — 'ma pitom, I've got it'.

לֹא נָכוֹן!.usage: Not true! Flat contradiction. Normal volume in an Israeli argument; nobody is offended.

אֲנִי לֹא מַסְכִּים.usage: I disagree (m.). The neutral, workplace-safe version. 'Maskima' if you are a woman.

אַתָּה טוֹעֶה.usage: You're wrong (m.). Direct and completely acceptable between peers. Would be startling in English, is not here.

נוּ בֶּאֱמֶת.usage: Oh come on. Exasperation at something absurd or unfair. One of the most Israeli sounds there is.

דַּי, נוּ.usage: Come on already / stop it. Half-annoyed, half-affectionate. Said to a friend, a child, a whole room.

אִי אֶפְשָׁר!.usage: Impossible! Refuses a demand — the other half of the sentence is usually a bureaucrat's.

לֹא יְכוֹל לִהְיוֹת.usage: That can't be. Disbelief at news, not at a person.

אֵין מַצָּב.usage: No way — refusal or disbelief. Young and emphatic. Also the delighted kind: 'eyn matzav!' at news too good to be true.

מָה אִכְפַּת לְךָ?.usage: What do you care? (m.) Sharp. Pushes back on someone meddling; not for strangers unless you want a fight.

תַּפְסִיק.usage: Stop (m.). Blunt imperative — 'tafsiki' to a woman. Fine with friends and children, harsh with strangers.

אֲנִי מַעֲדִיף לֹא לַעֲנוֹת.usage: I'd rather not answer (m.). Formal and final, without insult. Useful with the interrogation questions above.

בְּכֵיף.usage: With pleasure / gladly. Accepts a favor request warmly. Also answers ma nishma: 'be-keyf' = chilling, all good.

דַּי.usage: Enough / stop. Also an intensifier: 'day tov' = pretty good. The parent's word, heard in every playground in the country.

בְּקִצּוּר.usage: In short / long story short. Opens the summary, or cuts your own rambling before someone else does.

בְּקֶטַע.usage: Sort of / in a way. Young slang. 'Be-keta shel' = kind of like.

אַתָּה מֵבִין?.usage: You know? / get it? (m.) Tag question that checks you are still followed. Sprinkled through speech like the English 'right?'.

בֶּאֱמֶת.usage: Honestly / truly. Adds weight to what follows. Without the question mark, it is emphasis rather than doubt.

אֲנִי לֹא בָּטוּחַ.usage: I'm not sure (m.). Honest hedge — 'betucha' if you are a woman.

תֵּן לִי לַחְשֹׁב.usage: Let me think (m.). Buys a beat without conceding the point. 'Tni li' to a woman.

נִתְרָאֶה.usage: See you. The standard goodbye when a next meeting is vague but assumed.

נֵדַבֵּר.usage: We'll talk. Ends a call. Often means nothing specific will be scheduled, and everyone knows it.

הָיָה כֵּיף.usage: That was fun. Said leaving a meal or an evening. Genuine, low-effort warmth.

תִּשְׁמֹר עַל עַצְמְךָ.usage: Take care (m.). 'Tishmeri' to a woman. Carries real weight here — it is not just filler.

דְּרִישַׁת שָׁלוֹם לַמִּשְׁפָּחָה.literal: a demand of peace to the family
דְּרִישַׁת שָׁלוֹם לַמִּשְׁפָּחָה.usage: Regards to the family. Often shortened to 'd"ash'. Expected once someone knows your family exists.

יוֹם נֶהֱדָר.usage: Have a great day. Slightly formal — a shopkeeper, an email sign-off.

לֵב זָהָב.usage: Generous, kind. Usually 'yesh lo lev shel zahav'. Identical to the English.

לֵב שֶׁל אֶבֶן.usage: Unmoved by suffering. Also identical to the English idiom.

לֵב שָׁבוּר.usage: Broken heart — romantic loss, or plain grief.

לְשִׂים לֵב.usage: To pay attention / notice. Extremely common and fully literal in use: 'sim lev!' = watch out / notice this.

בְּכָל לִבִּי.usage: With all my heart. Sincere and a bit elevated — a speech, a letter, a thank-you that matters.

רֹאשׁ גָּדוֹל.usage: Takes initiative, does more than asked. High praise — army-born and now everywhere in work culture. Note the trap: it does NOT mean arrogant.

רֹאשׁ קָטָן.usage: Does the bare minimum, avoids responsibility. The insult that pairs with rosh gadol. A real cultural fault line.

רֹאשׁ פָּתוּחַ.usage: Open-minded, quick. Praise for someone who adapts.

בְּרֹאשׁ וּבְרִאשׁוֹנָה.usage: First and foremost. Written and formal-spoken register — news, speeches.

פֶּה גָּדוֹל.usage: Someone who talks too much or cannot keep a secret. Same as the English.

לָשׁוֹן הָרָע.usage: Gossip — but with religious weight: a genuine sin in halacha, and the term is used seriously even by secular Israelis.

מִפֶּה לְפֶה.usage: By word of mouth. How a restaurant or a plumber gets found here.

אַרְבַּע עֵינַיִם.usage: In private, just the two of us. 'Bo nedaber arba eynayim' = let's talk alone. No connection to glasses.

עַיִן הָרָע.usage: The evil eye. Live superstition, not folklore: praise a baby and someone will add a deflection. Related: 'bli ayin ha-ra'.

לִתְקוֹעַ כַּף.usage: To shake on it — to seal a deal by hand. The handshake is the contract in the shuk.

יָד בְּיָד.usage: Hand in hand — together, jointly. Also the name of the shared Arab-Jewish school network.

אֹזֶן קֶשֶׁבֶת.usage: A listening ear. 'Natan lo ozen keshevet' = heard him out properly.

גַּב לַקִּיר.usage: Cornered, out of options. Same image as the English.

בָּשָׂר וָדָם.usage: Mortal, human, fallible. Biblical register — used to excuse human limits: 'he's only basar va-dam'.

עַקְשָׁן כְּמוֹ חֲמוֹר.usage: Stubborn as a donkey. Said about someone who will not budge. 'Chamor' alone is an insult — an idiot.

עוֹבֵד כְּמוֹ חֲמוֹר.usage: Works like a donkey (m.) — grinding, thankless work. A complaint, occasionally a boast.

סוּס עֲבוֹדָה.usage: The reliable one who carries the team. Compliment.

סוּס טְרוֹיָאנִי.usage: Trojan horse. Politics, security and computing alike — same borrowing as everywhere else.

חָתוּל בַּשַּׂק.usage: Buying blind. 'Al tikne chatul ba-sak' = don't buy without looking. The French 'chat en poche', the English 'pig in a poke'.

חָתוּל וְעַכְבָּר.usage: Cat and mouse — a chase, a game of evasion.

חַיֵּי כֶּלֶב.usage: A miserable life. Complaint, usually about work.

נִכְנַס לַגֹּב הָאֲרָיוֹת.usage: Walked into the hostile room. Straight out of Daniel; used for a hard meeting or an interview.

צִפּוֹר קְטַנָּה אָמְרָה לִי.usage: I heard through the grapevine, and I'm not naming the source. Identical to the English.

נְמָלָה חָרוּצָה.usage: A tireless worker. Compliment, with a hint of 'and never stops'.

שׁוּעָל עָרוּם.usage: Sly, clever — grudging admiration, not condemnation.

פִּיל בַּחֲנוּת חַרְסִינָה.literal: an elephant in a china shop
פִּיל בַּחֲנוּת חַרְסִינָה.usage: Clumsy, tactless. The bull is an elephant here; the shop is the same.

זִכָּרוֹן שֶׁל פִּיל.usage: Forgets nothing — including, usually, a grudge.

שָׁפָן נִסּוּי.usage: Guinea pig, test subject. 'Shafan' alone also means coward.

דָּג גָּדוֹל.usage: A big shot, someone who matters. 'Dag katan' is the opposite — a nobody in this room.

אֶרֶץ זָבַת חָלָב וּדְבַשׁ.literal: a land flowing with milk and honey
אֶרֶץ זָבַת חָלָב וּדְבַשׁ.usage: The biblical description of the Land (Exodus 3:8). Quoted sincerely, and just as often with irony at a bus strike.

מֶלַח הָאָרֶץ.usage: The decent, unglamorous backbone people. Same sense as the English.

גַּרְגִּיר מֶלַח.usage: Take it with a grain of salt — treat the claim with doubt.

לֶחֶם וּמַיִם.usage: The bare minimum, subsistence. 'Chai al lechem u-mayim' = scraping by.

גֶּזֶר וּמַקֵּל.usage: Reward and punishment — politics, negotiation, parenting.

יֵין יָשָׁן בְּקַנְקַן חָדָשׁ.literal: old wine in a new jug
יֵין יָשָׁן בְּקַנְקַן חָדָשׁ.usage: The same old thing repackaged. From Pirkei Avot; still deployed at every product launch.

רוֹקֵד עַל הַבֵּיצִים.usage: Treading very carefully around someone. The English walks on eggshells; Hebrew dances.

אָכַל אוֹתוֹ חַי.usage: Destroyed him — in an argument, an interview, a match.
